heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XCIX
card_summary: This chunk continues Campanini's annotation of the catalogus auctorum in Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, systematically identifying obscure kabbalistic authorities listed by Zorzi. Figures annotated include Abraham Abulafia (as "AbrahamAlaphia"), Joseph Gikatilla ("Ioseph Carnitole"), Abraham ibn Ezra, the amora Chamma ben Chanina ("Amai ben hanina"), Azriel of Gerona, Chay Gaon, and several otherwise unattested figures — Reuben Hispanus, Aaron Mechubal, Rabbi Jacob "de amictu," "Mair merut/Neburac," "Bona dies de massa auri," and Me'ir ben Todros. Campanini demonstrates that Zorzi's primary conduit for most names is Johannes Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica, while for the more obscure and pseudepigraphic figures the decisive source is the Commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah by the notorious pseudepigrapher Mosheh Botarel (14th–15th c.).
essay:
# Campanini's Annotation of Zorzi's Catalogus Auctorum: Entries 5–16

## Overview

Pages 99–103 of Campanini's *Saggio introduttivo* present a densely annotated commentary on the sixteen kabbalistic authorities listed in what Campanini calls the *catalogus zorziano* — Zorzi's enumeration of Hebrew and kabbalistic sources at the opening of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The central editorial task is identification: many names appear in deformed, Latinized, or composite forms, and Campanini systematically traces each back to its source, revealing the layered mediation through which Zorzi accessed the Kabbalah. Two transmission channels dominate: Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (1517) and the Commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah* by Mosheh Botarel.

---

## Entry 5: Abraham Abulafia (as "AbrahamAlaphia")

Campanini identifies the figure as the great Spanish ecstatic kabbalist Avraham Abulafia, well known in Italian Christian-kabbalist circles. Two transmission routes are noted: the Latin translations prepared by Flavius Mithridates for Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, and a separate translation made by the converted Jew Felice da Prato for Cardinal Egidio da Viterbo. Campanini observes that the mangled form "Alaphia" itself betrays the proximate source:

> La storpiatura del nome di Abulafia denuncia anche la fonte dalla quale la menzione proviene: ancora una volta si tratta del *De arte cabalistica* di Johannes Reuchlin.

<cite>p. 99</cite>

---

## Entry 6: Joseph Gikatilla (as "Ioseph Carnitole")

The Spanish kabbalist Yosef ben Avraham Gikatilla is similarly derived from Reuchlin's list in *De arte cabalistica* (where he appears as "Iosef filius Carnitolis"). Campanini registers a telling discrepancy: Reuchlin attributes the *Porte della giustizia* (Sha'are Tzedeq) to this author, but Zorzi places a book with a similar title elsewhere in his list among the anonymous works (*adespoti*), suggesting Zorzi did not make the connection independently.

---

## Entry 7: Abraham ibn Ezra (as "Abraham aben Azra")

Zorzi cites ibn Ezra's *Commentary on the Torah* at least once, specifically on the cherubim (Gen. 3:23), interpreting them as "forms." Campanini rules out Reuchlin as the source here because the passage does not appear in *De arte cabalistica* and because Reuchlin's orthography is "aben Ezra" not "aben Azra." He raises the intriguing possibility that Zorzi may have consulted a Flavius Mithridates Latin translation of ibn Ezra's Commentary on Genesis (Gen. 1:1–4:8) contained in a manuscript that passed through Venice before being destroyed in the Plymouth bombing of 1941.

---

## Entry 8: Chamma ben Chanina (as "Amai ben hanina")

This talmudic *amora* is connected in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* to the literature of the *Sefer ha-'iyyun*. Campanini notes that Zorzi may have had independent access to that literature, but that the specific identification between the mysterious "rabbi Chammay" of the *sifre 'iyyun* and the talmudic Chamma ben Chanina is traceable to Reuchlin.

---

## Entry 9: Azriel of Gerona (as "Azariel Garonensis")

The important Catalan kabbalist 'Azri'el of Gerona, known for his *Perush qedushah*, is most probably derived from Reuchlin, who oscillates between the spellings "Azriel" and "Oriel" and includes at least one citation actually belonging to Ya'aqov ben Ya'aqov ha-Kohen of Soria. Campanini notes no explicit citations of Azriel appear in *De Harmonia Mundi* itself.

---

## Entry 10: Chay Gaon (as "Haii Geon")

This pseudepigraphic attribution (*Chay Ga'on*) is associated with texts from the *Sefer ha-'iyyun* tradition. Crucially, this figure does not appear in *De arte cabalistica*, so Zorzi's knowledge must derive elsewhere. Campanini proposes the Commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah* by Mosheh Botarel as the likely vehicle.

---

## Entry 11: Reuben Hispanus — The Botarel Problem

With entry 11 the annotation shifts decisively to Mosheh Botarel as Zorzi's primary source for otherwise unattested figures. "Reuben hispanus" does not correspond to any known edited or manuscript kabbalistic text, and Campanini credits the identification solely to Botarel's Commentary, which repeatedly mentions a "Re'uven lo spagnolo" and attributes to him two kabbalistic works: a *sefer belimah* (referencing the *belimah* formula for the sefirot in *Sefer Yetzirah*, meaning roughly "without determination") and a *sefer ha-shulchan* ("Book of the Table"). Campanini is candid about the limits of the inquiry:

> La irresistibile e 'patologica' inclinazione di Botarel per la pseudepigrafia, assai nota, lascia poche speranze per una più precisa identificazione di questo e di altri personaggi citati nel suo Commento.

<cite>p. 101</cite>

---

## Entry 12: Aaron Mechubal (as "Aaron Mechubal")

Another figure (the designation *ha-mequbbal*, "the kabbalist," is embedded in the name itself) unattested outside Botarel's Commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah*, where he appears as the head of a Babylonian talmudic academy. Zorzi evidently read the epithet as part of the proper name.

---

## Entry 13: Rabbi Jacob "de amictu, vitta vel Binda regia"

This entry is particularly revealing of Zorzi's working methods. The elaborate Latin gloss — "of the garment, headband or royal diadem" — reflects Zorzi's own hesitation in rendering a Hebrew title into Latin. The figure is Ya'aqov ben Me'ir of Gijón (attested only in Botarel), to whom Botarel attributes a work titled *tzanif melukah* ("royal turban," from Isa. 62:3). Campanini explains the multiple Latin attempts:

> La parola tzanif può anche essere intesa come fascia, benda o diadema e spiega così le diverse proposte tentate da Zorzi per latinizzare questa dicitura integrandola, secondo un costume in uso anche presso gli ebrei, nel nome stesso dell'autore.

<cite>p. 102</cite>

---

## Entries 14–16: Me'ir of Rothenburg, Yom Tov / "Bona dies," Me'ir ben Todros

"Mair merut, Neburac" is shown to be a mangled double reading of the single name of the halakhic authority Me'ir of Rothenburg, as attested in Botarel with uncertain manuscript orthography — Zorzi misread one name as two. "Bona dies de massa auri" is traced to a Botarel attribution of a work titled *Ketem Paz* ("fine gold," from Cant. 5:11) to a rabbi Yom Tov: *Yom Tov* = "good day" = *Bona dies*, while *ketem paz* yields *massa auri* ("mass/heap of gold"). Finally, "Mair filius Theodori" is identified as Me'ir ben Todros of Toledo, cited in Botarel as *Meir ben Todros me-Toletola*, with Campanini noting that the punctuation in the printed editions of *De Harmonia Mundi* erroneously implies that Mosheh Botarel's father was named Me'ir — an error in editorial punctuation, not in Zorzi's text itself.

---

## Significance

This chunk functions as a philological autopsy of Zorzi's kabbalistic bibliography, demonstrating that the Venetian friar's knowledge of Hebrew sources was mediated overwhelmingly through two texts: Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* for mainstream kabbalistic authorities, and Botarel's Commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah* for the more obscure and pseudepigraphic figures. The pattern of deformation in names — composite attributions, titles absorbed into author names, misreadings due to manuscript ambiguity — illuminates the material and linguistic distance between Zorzi and his sources, while Campanini's detective work establishes that Zorzi's access to Hebrew was real but constrained, dependent on intermediary compilations rather than direct reading of primary kabbalistic texts.
